title: Chapter 12: The Crackdown
content: The morning after Sterling's office confrontation, Chloe woke to find her door locked.
Not the slightly-ajar, technically-not-a-prison door she'd had for eleven days. A deadbolt. Installed overnight. She could hear the drill holes in the frame, still fresh, the metal still cold.
Sterling had escalated.

At 8 AM, Holloway arrived — not for morning rounds, but for a "treatment protocol adjustment." She was accompanied by two orderlies Chloe had never seen before. Both men. Both bigger than the previous ones. Both with the same dead-eyed professional neutrality that Chloe recognized from Vasquez: former military, now private security. Sterling had called in reinforcements.

"Effective immediately," Holloway said, reading from a clipboard, "Patient 17-C is under enhanced observation protocol. All group therapy privileges are suspended. All common area access is suspended. All non-clinical social contact is restricted."
"You're putting me in solitary."
"We're adjusting your treatment environment to better support your recovery."
Chloe looked at the orderlies. At the freshly-drilled deadbolt. At the camera that had been watching her for eleven days and was now, she realized, the only window to the outside world she had left.
"And my medication?"
Holloway's smile was almost apologetic. "We'll be administering your medication under direct observation. Effective immediately."
Translation: no more cheeked pills. No more hollow plant spine. No more pretending to swallow.
Not in her journal — the journal Holloway reviewed. On the back of a medication instruction sheet, the only paper she had left.
*They're putting me on observation protocol. Locked door. Direct medication. No contact. This might be my last clear-headed message for a while.*
*If I go quiet, don't stop. The article is out. Anna is pushing. The database is in Vasquez's hands. The files Rachel memorized are real. You have everything you need to finish this without me.*
*Layla — you're the smartest person in this building. If I'm compromised, you lead.*
*Sophia — your eight months of surveillance are worth more than any of us realized. Use them.*
*Maya — you're the strategist. Keep everyone from doing something stupid.*
*Vasquez — you're the inside line. Protect them.*
*And Rachel — you've been waiting three years. Don't wait anymore.*
*They think drugging me will stop this. They're wrong. This was never about me. It was about all of us. And all of us are still here.*
She folded the paper into a square the size of a postage stamp and tucked it into the seam of her mattress. Vasquez would find it during the next room check. Vasquez checked everything.
The medication arrived at 2:00 PM.
A paper cup. Three pills this time — the white one, the pale blue one, and a new one. Small. Yellow. Holloway held the cup and the water and watched.
"Open your mouth after. Tongue check. This time, we'll be more thorough."
Chloe looked at the pills. Then at Holloway. Then at the camera — the same camera she'd been smiling at for eleven days, the same red heartbeat that had become almost a companion.
She put the pills in her mouth. Drank the water. Swallowed.
Opened her mouth. Lifted her tongue. Holloway leaned in — closer than she'd ever leaned before — and inspected.

"Good," Holloway said, and Chloe heard something in her voice she hadn't heard before. Relief. Holloway had been worried. Holloway had been scared that Chloe would fight the medication too, the way she'd fought everything else.
But Chloe had learned something in eleven days: you don't have to fight every battle. You just have to win the ones that matter.

She'd swallowed the pills. Really swallowed them. Because fighting the medication today would get her restrained and injected, and restrained and injected meant she'd be unconscious, and unconscious meant she couldn't think, and thinking was the only weapon she had left.
Holloway left. The door locked behind her. The camera blinked.
Chloe lay on her bed and waited for the medication to hit.
She didn't know what the yellow pill was. She'd researched the white one and the pale blue one — SSRI, mood stabilizer — but the yellow one was new. She'd find out soon enough.
Twenty minutes later, the fog rolled in.
